item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn't get it. +1

Very nice. I'd never give a skip -1 just cause I'm unread. Made me feel kinda inadequate not getting the reference, but I actually avoid King's work, can't stomach the [redacted], so all references to him I'd only get 2nd hand anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I owe my life to this article. No, it should be cherished, put away in a museum. Nobody gets to touch this beautiful, beautiful thing. It's too good for us.

+1 with the force of a thousand self-driving cars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And Christine was a Plymouth Fury

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Christine was a Plymouth Fury. Stupid overly-relevant task force names :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On ██/██/2019, a space rocket fired by the ██████ company in 2018 with a █████ brand vehicle onboard was confirmed to be undergoing a travel path unintended by the ██████ corporation

Nitpick: this line waffles between company and corporation (and whichever word is used should probably be capitalized).

Also, PSA: Trucks by Steven King came out in 1973, ten years before this is estimated to have started. The 1983 Steven King novel about evil sapient cars trying to kill people is Christine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Earlier this year Elon Musk stuck a Tesla on a SpaceX rocket because he can and launched it into space so a Tesla roadster can float around in our solar system for a few thousand years and confuse the next sentient species that drops by.

item.title: Re: Explanation
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the answer!

I did actually know all of that minus the fact that the driver committed suicide and the rottweiler bit (in the Dark Tower series king presents him as being out of his mind on drugs), and personally I don't find it really makes it a particularly interesting piece, so sticking with my vote.

item.title: Explanation
item.content:encoded:
It's nothing major, really.

In 1973, Steven King wrote a story, Trucks, which was included in his first story collection, Night Shift. It was later adapted into the motion picture, Maximum Overdrive, written and directed by King himself.

In Trucks, vehicles attain sentience and kill humans. Realizing almost too late that they still need humans to pump fuel, they manage to force several into slavery. One of the killed humans is struck by a car, knocking him into a drainage ditch.

In 1999, Steven King was hit by a minivan, knocking him into a ditch. He survived with considerable injuries. He has since recovered, though the damage to his hips makes it hard for him to sit and write.

(In Real Life, the reason King was hit was because the minivan's owner was dealing with a rambunctious Rottweiler dog. But Trucks works better than Pet Sematary for this, anyway.)

The van was bought by King, slated for a charity event in which donors could pound on it with a sledgehammer.

The driver committed suicide in 2000, saying he couldn't face another winter.

That's about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I'm missing something major here.

I get that this is a reference to Stephen King, but is there a joke beyond that that I'm not understanding, or is it just a reference?

Has to be -1 for now, but I'll happily invert it if someone points out humour I've been dumb enough to overlook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting because this doesn't stand on its own if you don't get the joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The punchline in the Garfield skip wasn't about Jim Davis specifically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I had no fucking clue what this article was remotely about. Checking the discussion, I was confused about what made this funny. Learning this is supposedly an article about Stephen King felt out of no where. Why doesn't it say that in the description that is the target? I mean, the Garfield skip said Jim Davis by name without being all vague about it.

I also don't really get the last part. Why is a car on a rocket? What does it have to do with the asteroid? I'm clearly missing something here.

Sorry, but down vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't even a cursory glance at SCP-4070-A's identity give away the game? Or is this version of the Foundation completely staffed by people too young to remember what books were?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Speaking as the guy who refused to completely remove the concept of redshirts from his damned Star Trek skip, I think it would be more conspicuous if we all got the reference, while an entire institute of supernatural activity nerds somehow missed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's okay, guys. We all know King's house is well-protected by a scary lamp monster! Ooooooooohhh!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Delete this it was a mitsake!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Correct. SCP-XXXX-A cannot use self driving cars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP articles are scientific reports allowing the researchers to gain clear information about the anomaly in question. Unless there are some in-universe reasons that stop people from directly describing -A, it should at least say something like "SCP-4070-A is a human male" or otherwise state what SCP-4070-A is. Saying it is the target isn't enough (not even necessary, imo, since the context already says automobiles will move towards it)
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, if the distance between 4070-1 and -A is 0 (with King being on board, I guess?) does it have 100% chance to stay put, or…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The anomaly makes autonomous vehicles try to drive towards the home of Stephen King, who wrote a short story in 1983 called Trucks about a bunch of vehicles becoming autonomous and homicidal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a complete ignoramus, can someone explain the "joke" to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could have also done 04401, that's his zip code :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"straight line ^relative to curvature of the Earth"

Isn't technically correct, since "relative" is ambiguous in what it could mean… I assume you mean great circle route, which is only straight in that "It lies in a plane that intersects the sphere's centre".

Other options:

"great circle route ^the shortest course between 2 points across the surface of the Earth"

"straight line ^great circle route"

Or, you might decide the current way is most readable, but to me I was just like "that doesn't make sense…"

(If you actually meant "straight line ^as drawn on a (mercator projection) map" then say that; my point is the current footnote doesn't have a meaning.)

(You could also say it changed from "straight line on map" to "great circle route" when Google maps updated to show a globe, since before that the cars didn't understand they were on a sphere :p)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you're killing me
you really are
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a sec, is that suggesting…?

Goddammit, Musk. Stop trying to murder this one guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh, not really that funny to me.

-1

item.title: Re: lesbian_gengar: maybe the car crash was an attack. maybe the fucking cars they saw he knew their plans against them.
item.content:encoded:
maybe allude to the fact that 4070-A wrote something in 1983 about an autonomous hostile car?

Seconding this. It's a great joke, but I honestly didn't see what was going on until I came to the comments, so I'm no-voting on that basis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The execution of this alone is worthy of an upvote. So very well done and so very funny.

item.title: lesbian_gengar: maybe the car crash was an attack. maybe the fucking cars they saw he knew their plans against them.
item.content:encoded:
It could stand to be a little less subtle about who the target is, and at a minimum it could stand to clarify before the bit about the actual car accident that the target is a human, but otherwise I like it.

Maybe uncensor the exact date of the car crash and say something like "SCP-4070-A is a 71-year-old human male" or something like that. If you're feeling especially frisky, maybe allude to the fact that 4070-A wrote something in 1983 about an autonomous hostile car?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, no. I wish I was clever enough to think that. 419 and 70 are one off from "funny numbers" and a455 is hex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is asteroid-41970-A455 alternatively named after King?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey this is brilliant and funny and clever and I love its light energy. It's like cold iced tea on a hot August day. That's the energy this SCP has. My instinctive thought of what to do with this SCP to pass it out as refreshments to a group of people helping me do yardwork.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
me, reading this: this is just maximum overdrive by Steven King
me, reading it takes place in maine: wait
me, seeing the 1999 accident: hold on
me, checking the author: oh fuck you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Photo courtesy of http://www.terraprints.com/ released under a CC license.

Thank you to everyone in the SCPDeclassified discord who riffed on this idea, kinchtheknifeblade for suggestions and crit, and The Great Hippo for real vital crit and also a fair bit of wording.

ALSO THANK YOU TO scarhaver FOR THE TITLE IT REALLY PULLS THIS TOGETHER

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of me is just desperately worried people won't understand how sincerely great a joke this is and take it all at face value. I mean, yes, at face value this is great. But if you just take a look beyond the veil. Well, you'll see the face of the true double King.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess the Foundation considered it.
